dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 for a reported chest pain at 1734 Monroe Ave, Apt L, [CITY].
Hi, I'm Steve and I'm an EMT here for the rescue team. What made you call 911 today?
Yeah, I have a pain right in the middle of my chest, feels heavy and goes into my left arm. It's a 6 out of 10 right now.
Can you open your eyes and tell me your name, please?
Sure, I'm Richard.
How about where you are? Can you tell me the city you're in?
[CITY]. I think so, yeah.
What is the date today, sir?
March 29, 2020. I remember because today is a Monday.
Could you tell me how you're feeling right now?
Like a brick is on my chest. But oriented and alert.
Can you speak in full words? Is there any difficulty breathing or feeling like something is stuck in your throat?
Yeah, I can talk just fine, and my throat doesn't feel blocked or anything like that. Just a bit shaky I guess from the stress.
Let's see about your breathing. Any trouble catching your breath or doing so faster than normal?
He's breathing at 24 breaths per minute. Breathing is non-labored, but accessory muscles are slightly engaged in effort.
You mentioned your hands are shaking, is that because of the anxiety or something else you've experienced before?
Pulse is 115 bpm, strong and regular on the radial side. Skin warm, dry and normal color.
When did you last have something to eat or drink before this started? What were you doing then?
I had a sandwich a while back, and my chest started hurting since last night. I don't really remember what exactly happened to cause it, just woke up and felt this weight on my chest.
And were you doing anything different at the time you first noticed the pain, anything at all? Maybe activities or stress?
Not really. I was just lying down, watching TV when I noticed the chest tightness. It's been constant since then.
Is there anything that seems to make it better or worse at all? For example, sitting up or moving around, exertion, anything like that?
No, it's just there all the time. Nothing makes it better or worse.
How would you describe the pain? Like a burning, stinging, sharp stabbing, or is it more like pressure or tightness?
It feels heavy, like a brick is sitting over it. There's no sharpness, just pressure.
Does the pain go anywhere besides your chest-like into your back, shoulder, arms, or head? Could you describe that for us?
Yeah, it shoots into my left arm, and my arm feels numb too.
Blood pressure is 202 over 109. Pt may be hypertensive, need to note on chart for further confirmation in ED.
4-lead ECG patch applied successfully. Instruments are functioning, stay with me, Richard.
4-lead ECG is showing sinus tachycardia. Pulse is strongly coordinated. Maybe related to the pain or stress.
Pulse ox is at 97 percent, normal. No signs of hypoxia noted.
Do you mind if I give you some medicine now, sir? It's 324 mg aspirin, and it might help with the chest pain. Have you taken this before?
Yeah, I took a baby aspirin a few hours ago, but it didn't help much.
Blood glucose check - zero; can't be measured. Since you haven't eaten much recently, it's hard to determine levels accurately.
Sir, may we look at your skin a bit more closely? Just a routine check to evaluate pain response in general.
Oh, sure, go ahead. I saw you earlier.
Skin color and feel are normal-as expected. Dry and warm to the touch, no catchiness.
Do you have any pain or discomfort in your head or neck? Any headaches, dizziness, or stiff neck recently?
I haven't had a headache, and I don't feel dizzy or anything like that. Just my hands that shake occasionally.
Just to confirm, are you AAOx4, Mr. Richards? Can you tell me your name, location, the date, and why you're here?
I'm Richard. I'm in [CITY]. The date is March 29, 2020. I'm here because of my chest pain and shortness of breath.
Are you short of breath in any way right now?
Yes, I feel like I'm having trouble catching my full breath, like my lungs can't take in as much as normal.
12-lead ECG applied as per protocol. Patient is cooperative with this procedure. Let me let you know the results.
Per our findings, we're applying the medical chest pain - cardiac suspected protocol, standing order. Documentation for this is currently in place.
Let's recheck. Pulse is now 118, just a bit up, still sinus tach on ECG. Resp is 20 and decent depth, non-labored. BP is now 194 over 109. SpO2 is 99 percent. Good improvement.
Second 12-lead ECG taken now. Same findings as before-sinus tach with no STEMI or signs of acute myocardial infarction.
Richard, what destination hospital would you prefer to go to? We recommend [COMPANY_NAME] for best care with your condition.
Yeah, that hospital is close. I'm okay with going there.
We're going to move you onto the stretcher now, okay? We need you to walk with us and keep steady, if you're able.
Patient ambulated with a steady gait onto transport stretcher. He's been placed in a position of comfort and secured with five-point seatbelts for transportation.
Prior to transport, we've completed our assessments and updated vitals. [COMPANY_NAME]'s emergency team has been notified of the incoming patient status.
I'm going to double-check the restraints now. Once we're set, we'll begin movement to the waiting ambulance. All good to go.
Richard, just to note for our records, did you have alcohol usage recently? Any use over the last few days or today?
I did have 12 12oz beers the past two days. But didn't have any today, which is rare for me.
And have you ever had any kind of seizures or hallucinations as a result of alcohol use?
I've dealt with one seizure before, during a withdrawal period while not getting my alcohol. But I haven't had it in a while.
Do you have a history of high blood pressure? And are you on any medications for it?
I have been told I have high blood pressure before, but I haven't been compliant with any of the meds I was given.
And are you allergic to any medications?
No, I don't remember being allergic to anything. The aspirin didn't make me sick or anything like that.
Ambulance is loaded with patient, patient is secured with restraints, and all monitoring equipment is in place. En route now to [COMPANY_NAME].

Let the receiving ED know we're en-route with a male patient, 49 years old, active chest pain, 6/10 constant, radiating to left arm, non-responsive to single 81mg ASA taken earlier, currently on 324mg ASA and monitoring. No changes in status reported.
All the monitoring is stable. Vital signs trending are normal. Should arrive in about 30 minutes. Let's continue CP monitoring through this journey.
Are you still feeling the same? Do you have any shortness of breath or new symptoms since we began transport?
I'm still feeling the chest and arm, but no other issues. I can speak, move, and feel better now. Like the aspirin helped a little.
That's good to hear. Make sure to let me know if any symptoms change or increase in severity. The ECG is still stable and not showing ST changes.
[CITY] is almost in front of us. We'll get you settled on the ED bed. They'll take over care here. You did well during transport and your cooperation is admirable.
We're arriving now. Remember, you're not alone. We will stay until they have raised the rails and safely assist you to the bed.
Thank you, really. I've been through a tough time, but your help has given me a calm that I didn't have before.
Glad to help, and stay strong. We'll contact the ED nurse now for handover and confirm the patient is in good hands.

Report transferred to ED nurse. Patient condition remains stable with no new or worsening symptoms. Transportation completed successfully. Thank you to everyone involved for a smooth transport experience.
Rich, just to confirm your responsiveness, you were oriented to person, place, time, and situation with a GCS of 15.
Yeah, I understand all that. You all did a great job keeping me calm and walking through it.
Okay, and when you shared that you've had prior seizure-like episodes from withdrawal, was that when you last regularly consumed alcohol?
Yeah, that was when I was drinking daily before. Now I'm trying to stay off it as best as possible.
And your hand shaking-did it happen during the withdrawal or specific times in your daily life now?
Mostly during withdrawal periods, which is a known pattern with my drinking history.
